rationale: Several visual cues point to a moderately steep pitch in the 7:12 range. First, shadow casting: the home casts very prominent, elongated shadows extending well to the west-northwest of every roof plane — using the 50 ft scale bar (≈270 px), the ground shadows from the main eaves appear to extend roughly 18–25 feet beyond the building footprint, consistent with a ridge height and slope indicating ~7:12. Second, roof-to-eave geometry: the visible ridge lines across the complex hip-and-valley roof system appear notably narrow relative to the overall building footprint, suggesting significant elevation gain from eave to ridge — characteristic of pitches above 6:12. Third, the home's multiple intersecting hip roofs, valley intersections, and prominent peaks protrude visibly in plan view, indicative of a steeper architectural roof. Fourth, geographic/climate context strongly supports this estimate: Highland, Utah is an upscale foothill community with a northern continental climate and a strong tradition of steep-pitched architectural homes (7:12–9:12). The roof complexity and shadow prominence together point to 7:12 as the dominant pitch, though 6:12 or 8:12 cannot be entirely ruled out given the oblique overhead view.
